name: Surds and Indices — Powers and Roots Made Simple
whatIsIt: An INDEX (power) is repeated multiplication: 2⁵ means 2 multiplied five times. A few laws do all the work: to MULTIPLY same-base powers you ADD the indices (aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ); to DIVIDE you SUBTRACT (aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ); a power of a power MULTIPLIES ((aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ); anything to the power 0 is 1; and a negative power means 'one over' (a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ). A SURD is a root that isn't exact, like √2 — and √a = a^(1/2).
whyNeeded: Indices power scientific notation, growth, compound interest, and computer science. Surds and indices appear directly in SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude papers and underpin all of algebra — mastering the five laws makes huge expressions collapse to one line.
realLifeUses: Scientific notation for very big/small numbers (3 × 10⁸ m/s)
Doubling and growth: 2ⁿ bacteria, chessboard grains
Square roots in the Pythagoras theorem and distances
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: In aⁿ, a is the base and n is the index (power/exponent) — how many times a is multiplied.
Same base, multiplying → ADD the indices.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1.
An irrational root like √2 or ∛5; √a = a^(1/2).

story.title: The Doubling Rice Puzzle
story.text: Anaya reads that a chessboard doubles rice every square: 2, 4, 8, 16… 'How do I write square 10?' Her teacher says: 'That's 2¹⁰. And here's the magic — 2³ × 2² is not messy: same base, so ADD the powers: 2³⁺² = 2⁵ = 32. To go the other way, 2⁵ ÷ 2² subtracts: 2³ = 8. A power of a power multiplies: (2²)³ = 2⁶ = 64. And 2⁰ is just 1.' Anaya realises the five index laws turn scary powers into simple addition.
story.extractedProblem: Simplify 2³ × 2² using the index laws, and give its value.
story.answerInStory: 2³ × 2² = 2³⁺² = 2⁵ = 32 (add the indices when multiplying same bases).

visual.caption: Multiplying same-base powers just lines up more factors, so you ADD the indices: three 2's times two 2's makes five 2's = 2⁵.

teachingGallery.title: The five index laws
teachingGallery.note: ×: add. ÷: subtract. Power of power: multiply. a⁰ = 1. a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ.

standardMethod.summary: Match the operation to a law: multiply→add, divide→subtract, power-of-power→multiply, zero power→1, negative→reciprocal.
steps: Multiplying same bases: aᵐ · aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ (add indices).
Dividing same bases: aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ (subtract indices).
Power of a power: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ (multiply indices).

alternateMethods.name: Expand and count
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you forget a law — just write the factors out.
steps: Write each power as repeated multiplication.
Count the factors of the base to read off the new index.
Power of a power → multiply indices: (3²)² = 3⁴.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 is 1, so 7⁰ = 1.

trickPractice.intro: Match the operation to the law and read off the new index.

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying the indices when multiplying same-base powers (writing 2³ × 2² = 2⁶).
commonMistakes.fix: When you MULTIPLY same bases you ADD the indices (2³⁺² = 2⁵ = 32). You only MULTIPLY indices for a power OF a power, (2³)² = 2⁶.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 is 1, not 0. (Check the pattern: 2³=8, 2²=4, 2¹=2, 2⁰=1 — each step ÷2.)

questions.q: When you MULTIPLY same-base powers, the indices are…

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the index laws: show 2³ × 2² = 2⁵ (add), 2⁶ ÷ 2³ = 2³ (subtract), (2²)³ = 2⁶ (multiply), and 5⁰ = 1. Explain why multiplying adds the powers by writing out the factors.

revisionCard.summary: aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ (×→add), aᵐ÷aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ (÷→subtract), (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ (power→multiply), a⁰ = 1, a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ, a^(1/2) = √a. Don't multiply indices when you're just multiplying — that's only for a power of a power.

gameMission.title: Power Tower Climb
gameMission.brief: Climb the tower of powers! Add, subtract or multiply the indices with the right law, snap roots to whole numbers, and dodge the '2³×2² = 2⁶' trap.

realLifeProject: Make a doubling chart: 2⁰, 2¹, 2², … up to 2¹⁰. Notice how fast it grows, and check that 2³ × 2² lands on the same square as 2⁵.

rememberIt.unpack: Same base rules the indices. Negative power = reciprocal. √a = a^(1/2).

studentQuestions.q: Why does multiplying add the powers?
studentQuestions.a: Because 2³ × 2² just puts three 2's next to two 2's — five 2's altogether — so the new power is 3 + 2 = 5.

studentQuestions.q: Why is anything to the power 0 equal to 1?
studentQuestions.a: Follow the halving pattern down: 2³=8, 2²=4, 2¹=2, so 2⁰=1. Dividing by the base each step lands you on 1.
